text: Robert Bates, 73, shot and killed a man named Eric Harris while playing a game of "cops and robbers" with real police. Bates was a reserve deputy sheriff who volunteered his time and worked full time in the community. He had taken part in over 100 operations with the violent crimes task force and had donated money and resources to the department. On the day of the shooting, Bates thought he was using a Taser on Harris, but instead fired his gun and killed him. The police department has 130 reserve deputies, many of whom are wealthy and donate to the department. The shooting raises questions about the role of volunteer police officers and the potential dangers of giving special access to "real" policing. The department has since ended its relationship with Bates and is reviewing its policies regarding reserve deputies.

Robert Bates, a 73-year-old reserve deputy sheriff in Tulsa, Oklahoma, accidentally shot and killed a man during a training exercise. Bates, an insurance company CEO, was participating in a "cops and robbers" game with real police officers when he mistakenly grabbed his gun instead of a Taser. The man, Eric Harris, was tackled by deputies after selling an undercover officer a gun, but Bates claimed he thought Harris was running away and posed a threat. However, video footage showed Harris was not running and did not appear to be a danger to anyone. Bates' actions have raised questions about his qualifications and the use of volunteer police officers. While some departments have auxiliary programs that allow volunteers to assist with non-enforcement activities, Bates' involvement in a high-level training exercise raises concerns about the level of supervision and oversight of these volunteers.

Bates thought he was using a Taser but instead fired a fatal shot. The victim, Eric Harris, was not posing a threat and was running away, raising questions about why Bates, a wealthy donor to the sheriff's department, was involved in the operation. The incident highlights the issue of wealthy individuals buying their way into policing, which undermines professionalism and raises safety concerns. text: The Metropolitan Police Service (MPS) has confirmed that it was notified of a burglar alarm at the Hatton Garden Safe Deposit Company on 3rd April, but did not respond. Acknowledging in a statement that the alarm call was registered on its computer-aided dispatch system, MPS said an investigation was underway to determine why the call was classified as not requiring a police response. Detectives are still identifying the owners of the ransacked safe deposit boxes and trying to contact them to learn what had been lost. The actual value of the stolen items has yet to be confirmed, but estimates run from several hundred thousand to £200m ($300m). Meanwhile, the Daily Mirror claims to have obtained CCTV footage of the robbery, which it says shows at least six people were involved in the heist. The police have not confirmed the veracity of the video and say they have not released any footage of the burglary.

text: British police are investigating a major heist that took place in London's jewelry district. The theft occurred at Hatton Garden Safe Deposit Ltd. over the Easter holiday and the value of what was stolen has yet to be determined. The police were alerted to the burglary alarm going off but did not respond as the call was deemed to not require police attention. The investigation is now looking into why this decision was made and whether it had any impact on the outcome of the incident. A former police official speculated that the loss could be as high as £200 million ($300 million), although other sources estimate it to be in the hundreds of thousands of pounds. The Daily Mirror claimed to have obtained CCTV footage of the robbery, showing individuals dressed as utility workers entering and leaving the building with large bags and what appeared to be drill equipment. The thieves may have had access to the vault for several days, as indicated by time-stamped images showing their presence on different occasions. The police have not released any video of the heist and cannot confirm the authenticity of the footage published by the Daily Mirror. The investigation is ongoing, with police attempting to identify the owners of the ransacked safe deposit boxes and determine the extent of the losses. The Hatton Garden area is renowned for its diamond trade and has been the center of London's jewelry industry for centuries.

text: Thieves broke into the Hatton Garden Safe Deposit Ltd. in London's jewelry district over the Easter weekend and made off with an estimated £200 million worth of valuables. The heist was discovered on Tuesday when employees arrived at work and found the vault had been broken into. The thieves entered the building by disabling the elevator and climbing down the shaft to the basement, where they used a drill to bore through a 6-foot thick wall and gain access to the vault. The police have launched an investigation and are reviewing CCTV footage, which shows several individuals entering and leaving the building over the course of several days. The footage also shows the thieves using trash bins to remove the loot from the building. Police have not confirmed the exact value of the stolen items, but it is believed to include cash, jewels, and other precious goods. The incident has raised questions about the security measures in place at the safe deposit facility and how the thieves were able to carry out such a large-scale heist without being detected.
